data.title: Burlington raises 2025 EPS outlook to $9.19–$9.59 as Burlington 2.0 initiatives drive strong Q2 results
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Burlington Stores (BURL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michael B. O'Sullivan reported "very strong sales performance in the quarter," noting total sales grew 10% and comp store sales increased 5%. He emphasized, "These double-digit growth rates demonstrate the power of our business to consistently take market share even in an uncertain retail environment." O'Sullivan attributed the results to Burlington 2.0 initiatives, highlighting Merchandising 2.0, Stores 2.0, and the impact of new stores entering the comp base.

  • O'Sullivan stated, "Our operating margin expanded 120 basis points versus last year" and that EPS of $1.72 was "$0.42 above the high end of our guidance range."

  • He expressed caution for the back half, maintaining comp guidance of 0% to 2% for Q3 and Q4 due to concerns about external risks, including weather and tariffs. "We are raising our full year guidance and passing along most of the Q2 earnings beat. We are not flowing through the entire earnings beat. This is because of incremental tariff pressure in the back half."

  • CFO Kristin Wolfe said, "Total sales grew 10%, while comp store sales increased 5%, well above the high end of our guidance range. Traffic was flattish in the quarter. Our comp was driven by a higher transaction size."

  • Wolfe highlighted, "The gross margin rate for the second quarter was 43.7%, an increase of 90 basis points versus last year... driven by a 60-basis point increase in merchandise margin and a 30-basis point decrease in freight expense."

  • Wolfe reported, "We raised $500 million in additional term loan debt, primarily to fund the purchase of our highly automated West Coast distribution center," and that Burlington ended the quarter with approximately $1.7 billion in total liquidity.

Outlook

  • Wolfe detailed, "Comparable store sales are now expected to increase 1% to 2%, with total sales to increase 7% to 8% for the full year 2025."

  • Full year adjusted EBIT margins are projected to increase by 20 to 40 basis points, with updated adjusted EPS guidance of $9.19 to $9.59, up from $8.70 to $9.30 previously.

  • For Q3, comparable store sales guidance is flat to 2% growth, total sales increase of 5% to 7%, operating margin of down 20 basis points to flat, and EPS guidance of $1.50 to $1.60.

  • For Q4, comparable store sales guidance is flat to 2% growth, total sales increase of 7% to 9%, EBIT margin from a decrease of 10 basis points to an increase of 30 basis points, and adjusted EPS in the range of $4.30 to $4.60.

Financial Results

  • Wolfe reported, "Adjusted SG&A costs in Q2 decreased 30 basis points versus last year, driven by savings initiatives primarily in stores and leverage on higher comp sales. Q2 adjusted EBIT margin was 6%, 120 basis points higher than last year."

  • At the end of the quarter, "comparable store inventories were down 8% versus the end of the second quarter 2024. Our reserve inventory was 50% of our total inventory versus 41% of our inventory last year. In dollar terms, our reserve inventory was up 43% compared to last year."

  • The company opened 23 net new stores in Q2, for a total of 1,138 stores, and expects to open 100 net new stores in fiscal 2025.

Q&A

  • Matthew Robert Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about the "material drop off" in back half guidance after strong Q2. O'Sullivan responded, "This is just our standard playbook. We are planning and managing our business cautiously, and we are ready to chase a stronger trend... I don't want to understate the external risks... the most obvious risk is weather."

  • Boss also inquired about guidance puts and takes. Wolfe explained, "Tariffs for many countries are at a higher rate now than they were when we last issued guidance back in May. Our updated guidance assumes that we will offset most of but not all of this additional tariff pressure."

  • Irwin Bernard Boruchow, Wells Fargo: Queried about industry pricing and raising prices. O'Sullivan said, "We know that our customer is very, very price sensitive... the prospect of higher inflation, especially if it spreads to nondiscretionary categories like groceries, makes us very nervous."

  • Boruchow also asked about Q2 margin drivers. Wolfe stated, "The drivers of that expansion were really broad-based... lower shortage, combined with lower markdowns from faster turns, more than offset markup pressure from tariffs."

  • Lorraine Corrine Maikis Hutchinson, BofA: Asked about demographic trends. O'Sullivan reported, "Between the first quarter and the second quarter, we saw an improvement in trend in all demographic trade areas."

  • John David Kernan, TD Cowen: Inquired about inventory. Wolfe said, "Comp store inventory was down... 8% at the end of the second quarter... reserve inventory also gives us more flexibility."

  • Alexandra Ann Straton, Morgan Stanley: Sought details on store standards. O'Sullivan described improvements as "extraordinary," citing "customer service scores are running at historical all-time highs."

  • Brooke Siler Roach, Goldman Sachs: Asked about back-to-school trends. O'Sullivan said, "We've been very pleased with our back-to-school business this year. Early back-to-school selling in July was especially strong."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on guidance conservatism, tariff impacts, and pricing flexibility, displaying a slightly cautious to neutral sentiment.

  • Management remained confident in prepared remarks, emphasizing "very strong" results and flexibility, but were cautious in Q&A, stressing readiness to "manage our business cautiously and aggressively chase the trend."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism around macro risks and guidance remains, but management's tone is more optimistic due to recent performance. The phrase "we are very confident in our ability to execute it" signals strong internal confidence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full year comp store sales, total sales, EBIT margin, and EPS was raised from Q1, reflecting stronger Q2 results.

  • Management cited Burlington 2.0 initiatives as key to recent gains, expanding on specific impacts (e.g., Merchandising 2.0, Stores 2.0, and new store comp tailwinds), whereas Q1 focused more on navigating tariffs and consumer uncertainty.

  • Analysts continued to focus on macroeconomic risk, tariffs, and inventory positioning; however, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q1 to a more confident outlook in Q2.

  • Key metrics such as comp sales, gross margin, and inventory management showed marked improvement versus previous quarter, with more robust liquidity and expanded store openings.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted incremental tariff risk and potential weather impacts for the back half, stating, "Tariffs for most countries are higher now than when we last discussed earnings guidance on our Q1 call in May."

  • O'Sullivan underscored macroeconomic risks: "There are plenty of macroeconomic and other external variables that can have an impact on retail sales, higher employment, rising inflation, changes in consumer outlook."

  • Analysts raised concerns about the ability to offset tariff pressures, pricing power in a sensitive customer base, and implications of inventory strategies.

Final Takeaway

Burlington Stores closed Q2 2025 with robust sales and margin performance, driven by Burlington 2.0 initiatives, disciplined inventory and expense management, and strong execution in new store openings. Despite raising full-year guidance, management remains cautious given persistent tariff and macroeconomic risks but expressed confidence in the company's flexibility and ability to adapt to evolving market conditions, with ongoing investments in merchandising and store experience expected to provide continued support for growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Trip.com's marketing push is 'bearing fruit' - Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Shares of Trip.com (NASDAQ:TCOM) rallied on Thursday after the company’s latest quarterly results showed solid demand for leisure travel and the progress in the company’s increased marketing efforts.

With multiple growth engines, -- domestic travel, outbound travel, international expansion – and with increasing market share thanks to post-COVID tailwinds, Trip.com’s (NASDAQ:TCOM) high marketing intensity is “bearing fruit with sustained high growth,” Morgan Stanley’s Yang Liu wrote in Thursday’s research note to clients.

And the company has yet to see intensified competition despite noise in the market, Liu adds.

For the second quarter, the company realized a 16% increase in revenue from the same quarter last year with revenue from every segment (accommodation reservations, transportation ticketing, packaged tours, and corporate travel) improving from a year ago.

By segment, overall reservations at Trip.com’s (NASDAQ:TCOM) international online travel agency increased by over 60% from a year ago, while inbound travel bookings doubled. Additionally, outbound hotel and air ticket bookings surpassed pre-COVID levels by 120%.

“We are encouraged by the strong momentum across all segments of the travel industry,” said CEO Jane Sun, adding that the company’s strategy continues to focus on capturing growing demand from every demographic, with special attention to inbound travel.

The results coupled with the upbeat outlook for travel led Morgan Stanley to hike its price target for Overweight-rated Trip.com (TCOM) by 6% to $83, representing 27% upside from Wednesday’s closing price.

data.title: Vale fails to reach deal on Brazil railway concessions
data.description:
Vale (NYSE:VALE) resumes trading following a halt, +0.2% in Thursday's trading after the company said it did not reach an agreement with Brazil's National Land Transport Agency and federal government for new terms for licenses for the Carajás Railway and the Vitória-Minas Railway within established deadlines.

Vale (NYSE:VALE) said the concession contracts, which were pre-emptively extended until 2057 in 2020, remain in effect.

The company said it "remains committed to the general conditions of the renegotiation agreed upon in accordance with the terms of the agreement signed on December 30, 2024," and will fulfill all obligations set out in the contracts and adopt necessary measures to safeguard its rights.

data.title: Hong Kong regulator fines Deutsche Bank ~$3M for breaches
data.description:
Hong Kong's Securities and Futures Commission has fined Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB) HK$23.8M, or ~US$3M, for various regulatory breaches.

The breaches include overcharging clients on management fees, incorrect assignment of product risk ratings, and failure to disclose investment banking relationships in certain research reports.

The actions come in the wake of investigations done on the bank's December 2020 to December 2023 self-reports.

data.title: Tech Voices: Nvidia on Blackwell, AI job losses, Intel's Trump deal
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said the company selling its Blackwell AI chips in China is a "real possibility." 

“The opportunity for us to bring Blackwell to the China market is a real possibility,” Huang said on the company's quarterly earnings call, according to CNBC. “We just have to keep advocating the importance of American tech companies to be able to lead and win the AI race, and help make the American tech stack the global standard.” 

  • Stanford researchers said AI adoption is already having a significant impact on the availability of entry-level white collar jobs. 

Based on an analysis of ADP payroll records, the researchers found that employment in AI-exposed sectors such as customer service, accounting, and software development has declined 13% since 2022 for workers aged 22 to 25. 

“Early, large-scale evidence [was] consistent with the hypothesis that the AI revolution is beginning to have a significant and disproportionate impact on entry-level workers in the American labor market," the researchers said in the study, according to CNBC.  

  • Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) CFO David Zinsner said the company received $5.7 billion in cash on Wednesday as part of a deal with the Trump administration that will see the federal government take a roughly 10% stake in the chipmaker.

Zinsner said at an investor conference that the deal was intended to provide an incentive for Intel to maintain control over its foundry operations. The deal also gives the government a 5% warrant in case Intel's stake in the business falls below 51%. 

“I don’t think there’s a high likelihood that we would take our stake below 50%,” Zinsner said, according to Reuters. “So ultimately, I would expect (the warrant) to expire worthless."

data.title: Intel receives $5.7B of US stake from CHIPS grants: CFO
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) Chief Financial Officer David Zinsner said his company has received $5.7B of the U.S. government's 10% investment.

"This was a quick way to getting, initially, $5.7B in the door," Zinsner said on Thursday during a fireside chat at Deutsche Bank's 2025 Technology Conference in Dana Point, Calif. "By the way, we have received it. We got it last night. So that's on the balance sheet. So that was one thing that eliminated the need to access the capital markets in any other way in the near term."

The $5.7B came from part of Intel's share of the U.S. CHIPS and Science Act funding. Under the agreement, the U.S. is buying 433.3M shares at $20.47 each. Intel had already received $2.2B in grants from CHIPS at an earlier date. Another $3.2B will stem from the Secure Enclave program through the U.S. Department of Defense elevating the total investment to $11.1B.

"I think having the U.S. government invested in us and our success is absolutely helpful," Zinsner said. "I think it is helpful with customers ... Obviously, when you take the grant money and switch it over into equity, it does have a dilutive impact ... but when you run the math, it doesn't take a lot of volume of foundry wafers for us to make this accretive for us."

Interestingly, in the same week the Trump Administration confirmed it was taking a major stake in Intel, Japan's SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY)(OTCPK:SFTBF) also announced it would make a $2B investment in Intel common stock, paying $23/share.

"It was just a coincidence that it all fell in the same week," Zinsner said.

data.title: CDC vaccine advisory panel sets September meeting dates
data.description:
The CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP), which makes determinations on the segments of the population that should receive vaccinations, will next meet on Sept. 18 and Sept. 19.

A Federal Register notice posted Thursday states the two-day meeting will cover Covid-19, hepatitis B, the measles, mumps, rubella, varicella (MMRV), and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) vaccines.

The seven-member panel is composed of individuals that have been assigned only since June. That month, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. fired the prior members, replacing them with individuals personally vetted by himself. 

The announcement also comes less than a day after the White House fired CDC Director Susan Monarez, saying she was not following the president's or the Make America Health Again agendas. Also on Wednesday, the U.S. FDA authorized Covid-19 shots for the upcoming season, but with restrictions.

Recommending Covid shots for the groups the FDA identified is up to ACIP, and its decision will have ramifications for whether insurers cover the jabs. 

ACIP is chaired by Martin Kulldorff, a biostatistician and former Harvard Medical School professor who spoke out against vaccinating children against the virus, mask mandates, and lockdowns during the pandemic. 

CDC is allowing both written comments and oral comments to be made at the meeting from the public. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Wall Street inches higher after a slew of economic data and NVDA earnings results
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes are inching higher on Thursday, as investors weigh a wave of economic data and Nvidia’s (NVDA) latest earnings, a day after the S&P 500 secured a record close.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) finds itself near even. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has climbed 0.2%, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) moved higher by 0.5%.

From a sector-by-sector vantage point, six of the 11 S&P segments are up in the green with Communication Services and Info Tech leading the way. On the other end, Health Care has suffered the most so far on the day. 

Shifting to the bond market, the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) has moved down 2 basis points to 4.21%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) has moved higher by 2 basis points to 3.63%. Also, a $44B 7-year Treasury note auction tailed, indicating weaker demand for government-backed fixed-income securities.

On the economic front, Q2 GDP growth was revised higher to 3.3%, while the PCE increase was revised lower to 2.0%. 

Furthermore, initial jobless claims  unexpectedly fell in the past week. Q2 corporate profits bounced back, rising 2.0%. 

Additionally, pending home sales dipped 0.4% in July, which was weaker than economists had forecasted. 

Lisa Cook filed a lawsuit challenging President Donald Trump's move to oust her from the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, escalating the conflict between the White House and the U.S. central bank.

Nvidia (NVDA) remains in focus as shares dip 1% after the firm reported fiscal second-quarter results and guidance that topped Wall Street's expectations and unveiled a massive share buyback program.

As for stocks that are on move, shares of Snowflake (SNOW) have surged 20.9% after the company produced a strong earnings report.

data.title: Top gaining Russell 2000 stocks as index close to strongest performance vs. large-caps
data.description:
The Russell 2000 (IMW) is close to its strongest relative performance compared to the S&P 500 (SP500) since last November.

Small-caps are already hitting their highest levels since March relative to their large-cap counterparts (IWB).

 

 

The Russell 2000 (NYSEARCA:IWM) has been mostly flat on Thursday and up 6.8% year-to-date.

data.title: Nike to cut corporate workforce by 1% - report
data.description:
As part of CEO Elliott Hill’s efforts to realign the business and “create the next great chapter” for the iconic sportswear company, Nike (NYSE:NKE) is reportedly planning to let go of 1% of its corporate staff, according to CNBC.

The latest job cuts follow Nike's (NYSE:NKE) initiative to realign the business into dedicated cross-functional teams by sport as part of its “Win Now” turnaround strategy.

The new structure shifts Nike’s focus from demographic categories, like Men’s, Women’s and Kids, into five fields of play – running, basketball, football, training, and sportswear -- with the objective of developing products for specific athletes and customers. As such, the company’s corporate structure will change from Consumer and Sport, Marketing, Product Creation, into three core brands, Nike, Jordan, and Converse.

By changing the alignment, Hill hopes to reinvigorate the innovation pipeline, which was stagnant under the prior leadership and blamed for the company’s anemic growth.

As part of these changes, some positions will be eliminated while some staff will be moved into new positions. Nike (NYSE:NKE) staff will learn if their position has been impacted by September 8 with the majority of new roles taking effect September 21. Accordingly, to “make space for these conversations,” corporate employees based in an office in the U.S, and Canada will work remotely during the week.

The news had little impact on Nike (NKE) shares which are holding onto a modest gain.
